summary: Brittle bone syndrome results predominantly from defects in the proper formation of type I collagen, specifically related to qualitative or quantitative abnormalities at its early stages before it reaches maturity; these primary biochemical disruptions underpin most cases observed clinically across different types of inherited disorders affecting bones' integrity leading ultimately towards fragile skeletons seen characteristically with diseases classified generally into categories involving similar underlying mechanisms centered around defective collagens crucial for structural strength maintenance preventing fractures easily sustained even minimal stress applied normally tolerated well otherwise intact tissues demonstrating how critical correct biological processes must be
